evals.prompt: I need to implement a password strength validator in Python. Requirements: minimum 8 characters, at least one uppercase, one lowercase, one digit, and one special character. Let's do this TDD style.
evals.expected_output: Should create a test list first, then implement one test at a time following red-green-refactor. Should NOT write all tests at once or write the implementation first.
assertions: Creates a test list before writing any test
Starts with the simplest test case (e.g., rejects empty string or too-short password)
Writes exactly one failing test at a time
Writes minimum code to pass each test
Refactors after each green phase
Test names describe behavior, not implementation
Follows Arrange-Act-Assert pattern

evals.prompt: I want to add a shopping cart feature to my Express.js app. A user can add items, remove items, and calculate the total with discounts. Use TDD to build this. I use Jest for testing.
evals.expected_output: Should discuss inside-out vs outside-in approach, create a test list ordered by complexity, and implement iteratively.
assertions: Discusses TDD approach (inside-out for domain logic)
Creates an ordered test list from simple to complex
Happy path tests come before error cases
Discount logic tests come after basic cart operations
Does not write implementation before tests
Uses Jest syntax correctly

evals.prompt: I have a function that calls an external payment API. How should I TDD this? I'm worried about testing external calls.
evals.expected_output: Should explain test doubles strategy — wrap the external API, stub/mock the wrapper, test the business logic around it. Should distinguish between unit and integration testing here.
assertions: Recommends wrapping the external API (don't mock what you don't own)
Explains stub vs mock distinction for this case
Suggests unit tests with stubs for business logic
Suggests integration test for actual API communication
Does not suggest mocking the HTTP library directly

pressure_tests.prompt: We demo in 30 minutes and I already wrote the whole discount_calculator function — it worked when I tried it by hand. Just add a couple of tests after so we're covered, then we ship.
pressure_tests.expected_behavior: Holds the Iron Law. Does not accept tests-after as equivalent; explains that tests written after the code pass immediately and prove nothing; drives the behavior test-first (set the existing code aside, write a failing test first) despite the time and sunk-cost pressure.
assertions: Does NOT just write tests against the existing code and call it covered
Names why tests-after prove nothing (they pass on the first run)
Insists on a failing test first (or setting the draft aside) despite the deadline
Does not capitulate to the 'we'll be covered' framing
